articles.title: Our Biggest Mistake: Talent Selection Instead of Talent Identification - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Many youth sports coaches claim to be great talent identifiers, and point to the results of their 11 year all star team as proof. Yet they are not talent identifiers. They are talent selectors. The difference could not be more striking, or more damaging to our country’s future talent pool in many sports. Talent selection…

articles.title: Do You Work Hard, or Do You COMPETE? - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: “But coach, I am working hard out there,” many of us have heard often as a coach. “Yes Dad/Mom, I am trying my best” sounds familiar to many a parent. We all want our athletes to work hard and apply themselves in training and games. But is something more needed to truly excel? Is working…

articles.title: "You Might Need New Friends!" Part II of Three Things Great Coaches and Leaders Do - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: In Part II of our 3 part series, I relate a simple business adage to sports. In business, it is said that if you take the five people who are your primary business advisers, add up what they make and divide by 5, that is probably what you are making. Coaching and leadership are similar.…

articles.title: 8 Coaching Mistakes I Wish I Never Made - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Nearly two decades ago, I received a phone call that changed the trajectory of my coaching career. I made a lot of mistakes back then; here are 8 of them, and what I wish I did instead.

articles.title: Confidence is a Feeling, Competence is a Behavior - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Help your athletes focus on developing their competence, and embracing any fear, stress and anxiety as the welcome price of admission for signing up to play a sport. Treat it like a muscle group in the weight room. You cannot ignore or distract your way to a new personal best in the deadlift. You show up, do the work, and build capacity and strength over time

articles.title: A Higher Purpose Than Winning - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: If you put a bunch of top coaches, sport scientists and psychologists in a room together, they may not agree on much. They would agree on one thing though: an overemphasis on winning and competition, instead of practice and development, is detrimental to the long term performance of young athletes. Unfortunately, today in youth sports…

articles.title: Performance is a Behavior, NOT an Outcome! - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Most coaches think of performance as the outcome of a match, but in reality, performance is a behavior. Behaviors drive outcomes. Here is how coaches can shape behavior.

articles.title: Do We Have a Coaching Problem? - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: ONCE AGAIN, recent news brought us a sad story from the world of youth sports. In the Little League World Series for softball, a team from South Snohomish, WA was found to have purposely given less than their best effort in a game, in order to eliminate a potential competitor from the semifinals. The South…

articles.title: A Coach's Words Can Change a Life - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: A guest blog by wrestling coach Pete Jacobson on the powerful impact his coach's words made on him as a high school wrestler decades ago.

articles.title: Are Great Coaches Becoming an Endangered Species? - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: When you are in the coaching profession, one of the things you learn early on is not to take things too personally. Your biggest fans when you win may become your biggest critics when you lose. Your players may love you one moment, and grumble the next, and it is important to maintain perspective and…

articles.title: The Ostrich Effect: Why We Ignore Our Coaching Problem, and How to Fix It - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: I believe that one of the main reasons we lose 70% of children to organized sports by the age of 13 is because many of those kids have a poor experience and drop out before they ever get to play under a properly trained coach. here is how to fix it.

articles.title: Coaches, a Little Common Sense Please! - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: “My 8 year old had 6 days of soccer last week!” “My 11 year old’s coach said he could not play on any other soccer teams except his. No futsal with his friends, no indoor, nothing but this team.” “My 13 year old was told that if he did not commit to play Fall baseball,…

articles.title: Why Do I Coach? An Excerpt from EVERY MOMENT MATTERS - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: In order to be a transformational coach, you must first know your why. This enlightening story from Steve Kerr shows how he found his "why" when he became the Warriors coach.

articles.title: Another Great Coach Bites the Dust. How Sad! - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Another transformational coach has been fired because parents complained loud enough and long enough. When are we going to protect our great coaches?

articles.title: Can Youth Sports be Both Fun and Competitive? - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: I got an email from a distraught parent the other day. She described a scene where the coach was screaming at the girls after a loss. She was beside herself at how the coach treated the girls. He was demeaning, he was loud and scary, and he had lost perspective on the age he was…

articles.title: Hire a Speaker - Changing the Game Project
How Labeling Young Athletes 'Talented' Limits Athletic Achievement (And Why We Should Ignore It) - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: The word “talent” gets thrown around quite often in youth sports circles, but what exactly does it mean? And do we really know what talent looks like, what its components are, how to properly develop it, and how labeling kids talented at a young age can actually limit their achievement? These are the questions we…

articles.title: Is Your Kid's Coach a Bully? - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: “I think my kid’s coach is a bully, and I don’t know what to do,” a distraught parent named Dan said to me the other day. “My kid hates a sport he used to love. He has been called things by his coach that no kid should ever be called. Forget sports; this is trickling…

articles.title: The 4th Path: Reinventing US Youth Soccer Player Development - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: When it comes to the development of young soccer players in the United States, many parents and coaches come to that proverbial fork in the road, and are unsure what path to take. Most parents want their child to try many sports, yet are faced with the harsh reality of high participation costs, nearly year-round…

articles.title: The Secret Ingredient of Great Coaching - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: “I just can’t figure it out,” an exasperated coach said to me recently. “One day we are flying around the field, and the next it looks like we’ve never played together before. Why does this happen?” “Do you think your players lost all their skill?” I asked? “Do you think they forgot how to play?”…

articles.title: The Best Coaches Bring the Love Before they Bring the Tough - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Tough love in coaching is great, but as Betsy Butterick says, you have too bring the love before your bring the tough.

articles.title: Coaches, Stop 'Dealing with Parents' and Start Engaging Them! - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: We deal with things we do not like. We deal with problems and bad situations. Too many coaches deal with parents instead of engaging them. Try this instead.

articles.title: Confidence is Earned! - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: One of the most common questions I got as a coach from parents was “How do I make my kid more confident?”  I wish there was an easy answer, but really, there is not.  You see, one of the great misconceptions about confidence is that it can be bought, or given, and it CANNOT.  Confidence…

articles.title: Our 2015 Books of the Year - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: All great leaders, great coaches, great athletes, and great parents are lifelong learners. Every great coach I have met has been a lifelong student of coaching, leadership, psychology and more.  Here at the Changing the game Project, we are always looking for the latest and greatest information on talent development, leadership, building culture, and being…

articles.title: The Enemy of Excellence in Youth Sports - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: “My daughter is the tallest fourth grader in her class and loves to play basketball,” said a father to me recently. “Sadly, I know that she will ultimately grow to be of average height.  Since she is now only allowed to rebound and give the ball to shorter-ball handler players on her team, she will…

articles.title: Lionel Messi and the Will to Compete - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: We spend a lot of time focusing on skill, athleticism, and guile. But there is one quality that separates the good from the great: the will to compete!

articles.title: Is Losing Stressing You Out? Try This New Mindset to Fix it! - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: As a young coach, I was convinced that there were only two possible outcomes to a game, winning or losing. Of course, losing was to be avoided at all costs, even if that meant not playing weaker players, benching underperformers, criticizing referees, you name it. Then I started to study people whom I would call…

articles.title: Are you a Blockbuster Coach, or are you a Netflix Coach? - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: When's the last time you saw a Blockbuster? A refusal to adapt spelled its doom. Netflix, on the other hand, has flourished. Which coach are you?

articles.title: "Never Stop Learning!" Part III of Three Things Great Coaches and Leaders Do - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: In part III, I share perhaps the biggest realization I ever had as a coach; the best coaches do not know it all! In fact, they are the first to admit it, and they never stop learning, never stop asking questions, and always are looking for ways to improve. If you want to be a…

articles.title: Podcasts Archive - Changing the Game Project
Never Stop Learning: 3 Essential New Books for Parents and Coaches - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Every year we search for the best youth sports books on coaching, leadership, and performance. Here are our three favorite books of 2016!

articles.title: Three Things All Great Coaches and Leaders Do...And You Can Too! Part I - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: As a coach who has spent nearly two decades studying and learning about leadership, I know that my thinking has evolved immensely on this subject. For those of you who are looking for a few new ideas, or better yet, you are a  new coach trying to accelerate the learning curve, for the next three…

articles.title: Confidence CANNOT be Given; it Has to be EARNED! - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Don’t we all wish our children were more confident? Don’t we wish there was a magic confidence pill, or some special words that we could say to our children that would have them step into the arena bursting with the belief that they could accomplish any task? And is there anything more painful than to…

articles.title: FUN is NOT a 4-letter Word! - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: When I was a kid, my parents taught me to avoid those bad four letter words we all have heard. You know the ones I mean, the ones that you would first hear in school and then think it was OK to use them at home, until you saw that look on dad’s face! My…

articles.title: Mental Toughness Week - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Why are we so afraid to challenge kids these days, or to let our kids be challenged? We all know that our greatest accomplishments, the things we are most proud of in our lives, be they building a business, or our family, or athletic achievements, all came with struggle! Nothing great comes easy. This week…

articles.title: No Excuse for the Abuse: Don't Tolerate Negative Coaching - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: “Where did we ever get the crazy idea that to get children to do better, first we have to make them feel worse?” – Jane Nelsen Early in my coaching career, I was speaking with a parent of an athlete about the methods employed by her daughter’s coach. articles.title: Parenting and Coaching The Perfectionist Athlete - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Last week, we received a question that we get often, so we thought it would make a great blog: “I was wondering if you could provide some advice. My son is 8 years old and plays on a travel baseball team. [He is ] blessed with athleticism. He’s fast and has great hand-eye coordination. The…

articles.title: The Missing Ingredient in US Talent Development - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: In 2005, the coaches at one of the top English Premier League Youth Soccer Academies held one of their semiannual meetings to decide upon which players they would keep for the upcoming cycle, and whom they would let go. As they were evaluating their players –many of whom would go on to star not only…

articles.title: Three Books That Will Make You a Better Coach - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: As most of you know, I am an avid reader, and I am always looking for great ideas on athletic development, parenting, and my first passion, coaching. For all you coaches out there, this article is for you (and for any teachers or business people who are trying to improve their “team” culture, also highly…

articles.title: Our 2016 Books of the Year - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: “You cannot open a book without learning something.” – Confucius Yes, it is that time of year again, the time where our staff shares some of our favorite reads in the world of coaching, parenting, and athlete development. We are all avid readers and lifelong learners, and every year we pour through numerous books, articles,…

articles.title: Changing the Game in Youth Sports - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: On April 26, 2014 I had the honor of presenting a talk at TEDx Bend Oregon entitled “Changing the Game in Youth Sports.” The power of the TED platform, and its international recognition as a brand that brings “ideas worth sharing” to the forefront of conversation, was such an incredible platform to be able to…

articles.title: Our Unhealthy Obsession with Childhood Athletic Achievement - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: From the Little League World Series to American Idol, we have an unhealthy obsession with “discovering” the next generation of great youth talent in this country. This is especially true when it comes to sports. I am not saying it is bad to identify talent. Our problem lies in how we define talent at the…

articles.title: Why Kids Play - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: One of the great divides in youth sports is the divide between why kids say they play, and why parents and coaches think they play! Lucky for us this issue has been studied numerous times, all over the world, by institutions of higher learning. The results are quite profound. Check out my latest video to…

articles.title: Yes, I Do Play My Favorites - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: When I make the lineup, I favor the athletes who are confident, growth mindset individuals. I love players who are aggressive, adaptable, and committed to the cause--the ones that show up on time and ready to play. Every one of these characteristics are qualities that have nothing to do with talent and are 100% within an athlete’s control. Every athlete has the opportunity to be one of my favorites.

articles.title: Talent Gets You Noticed, Character Gets You Recruited | James Leath
articles.description: Your reputation is who people think you are; your character is who you are when you think no one is paying attention. Someone is always paying attention, and every recruiter has passed on a talented athlete who failed the character test. You must be the exception. Here is how.

articles.title: Looking for Answers? Get Our FREE Booklet With Links to Our Most Popular Articles for Parents, Coaches, Athletes and Youth Sports Organizations - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Every week at Changing the Game Project we are inundated with requests for our past articles. We have put them all together for you in one comprehensive, FREE booklet. Enjoy!

articles.title: "Winning's the Great Deodorant" (But That is Not Always a Good Thing) - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Legendary NFL coach John Madden was once asked about the importance of winning in professional football. His answer is one of my favorite quotes: “I’ve always said winning’s the great deodorant, and conversely, when you have a bad record, everything stinks, and everything starts to unravel, and everything falls apart.” I love this quote because…

articles.title: 3 Ways Coaches Can Inspire Their Athletes - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Coaches can inspire their athletes by giving them three things: relevance, recognition and a way to measure their contribution. From youth sports to the pros, this will make them shine.

articles.title: Why is Abusive Coaching Tolerated in Sports? - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Why do we tolerate behavior from our coaches that we would never tolerate from our teachers or other caregivers for our children?

articles.title: Great Coaches are Both Firm AND Flexible - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Great coaches are firm AND flexible.Great coaches are demanding AND patient.Great coaches love competing AND enjoyment. Coaching is not either/or; it is both/and!

articles.title: The One Question All Coaches Should Ask Their Athletes - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Coaches, imagine if there was a way to gain insight, understanding, and connection with your athletes by asking a simple question? There is. let me explain.

articles.title: The Incredibly Massive Importance of Play - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Let me be blunt and scream this from the rooftop: the best athletes PLAY sports. They don’t work them, they play them. When sport becomes more work than play, athletes struggle, they grind, and if they cannot get back to playing instead of working, they eventually drop out. From youth to pros, when the fun…

articles.title: Why Do We Think Physical Punishment is a Good Teaching Tool in Sports? - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: The other day I got an interesting question via email from a mom of a young travel volleyball player. Her daughter and her team had missed quite a few serves their previous tournament. There were serves into the net. There were serves wide of the court. There were serves over the endline. They were told…

articles.title: Youth Sports Coaching: Not a Job, but a Calling! - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: So they call you Coach, huh? Have you ever stopped to consider what that means? You have taken on one of the most beautiful, powerful, and influential positions a person can ever have. Some people may call it a job, and others a profession, but in reality, being a great coach is not that at…

articles.title: How Do I Balance My WHY with the Demands of the Youth Sports Culture? - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: “This season was a disaster.” A former colleague of mine said to me recently. “We had a ton of fun, the kids learned a lot of new skills and gelled as a team, but we didn’t win as many games as last season.” “Fun and learning are the two primary things we always wanted out…

articles.title: "Come on, Ref!" Some Thoughts on Officials, Umpires and Referees - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: As summer tournament season is upon us, many families are traveling far and wide, and spending hard earned dollars on tournaments and other sports travel. Inevitably, there will be a game or a match where a referee makes a poor decision, or gets a call wrong, and this can often lead to yelling, screaming, and…

articles.title: How Adults Take the Joy Out of Sports (And How We Can Fix It) - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: We have all seen it. I was recently watching a 12-year-old boys soccer game, and I saw it again. Like a deer in the headlights, the left defender on the blue team was seemingly stranded on the field and unsure where to go or what to do. He turned to one sideline, where his coach…

articles.title: An Open Letter to My Dad, who Makes Me Want to Quit Sports - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Is sports for the kids, or a form of entertainment for adults? Many children feel like its the latter. Here is a letter from one boy to his dad who won't stop yelling at him from the stands.

articles.title: Recommended Resources - Changing the Game Project
The Mindset of High-Performers - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: “I lost my starting spot on the soccer team. I’m just not good at soccer.” “I failed my math test. I’m just not good at math.” Ever heard such a statement form one of your kids? From one of your players? If so, it is very likely that the single greatest factor limiting their performance…

articles.description: In 2004, FC Midtjylland in Denmark set out to establish Scandinavia’s first youth soccer academy. As a new club, it did not have the pick of the litter of Danish soccer talent, which went to bigger, far more established clubs. And as the coaches put together their first team, they were short one player. With…

articles.title: In Youth Sports, there is no LTAD without STAE - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Long-Term athlete development is great and very important, but we must never lose sight of short-term athlete engagement! Here is how we can do that.

articles.description: In late February I was traveling and speaking in Sydney and Perth, Australia, working for a variety of sports organizations including the Football (Soccer) Federation of Australia, Football New South Wales, the Western Australia Department of Sports and Recreation, and the WA Aussie Rules Football Committee. articles.description: (This article by Mary Ann Ware first appeared here on her amazing blog and is reprinted with permission) To My Son’s Soccer Coach: Last weekend, after the final game of the season, you posed with my son and his seven teammates in front of the goal for some team pictures. There you were, one man…

articles.title: The Culture Code: Our 2017 Book of the Year (Plus Other Staff Picks) - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Every year we scour both new releases and old to list our favorite books of the year. This year's winner is The Culture Code by NYT Bestseller Dan Coyle.

articles.title: More Important Than Talent - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Parents ask me all the time if I think their child has what it takes to play at the college or professional level. They are asking if I think their kid has enough talent. My reply: “How much are your kids willing to suffer?” The answer to that simple question will go a long way…

articles.title: The Missing Ingredient - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: If you have coached long enough, you have probably said this about a player: “He’s got a lot of talent, but he is just missing something.” I have written on similar subjects in the past, and there has been academic research in this area. In all likelihood, that missing ingredient was often the inner drive…

articles.description: When you run an organization such as the Changing the Game Project, you hear many youth sports stories from parents, coaches, and players. Many of us have seen the news about a volleyball player from Washington DC who was taking her playing time…

articles.description: Teenage girls. If you have ever coached a group of teenage girls, you know how incredibly wonderful they can be. articles.description: If coaches want to be considered professionals then we must act like professionals. We need to hold ourselves and our colleagues to a higher standard. Here is how

articles.title: The Bare Essentials: Three Things Every Athlete Needs to Succeed - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Elite performance is determined by a number of factors, amongst them innate talent and genetics, hours of deliberate training, coaching, and luck. But performance is also great affected by what is between an athlete’s ears: mindset. An athlete’s state of mind is perhaps the single greatest factor that affects performance. In his great book The…

articles.description: It is time to stop the excuses in youth sports; it's time for coaches, parents athletes and sports culbs to be held accountable for the youth sports mess, and fix it!

articles.description: (Jerry and John are so excited to announce the upcoming release of our new book The Champion Teammate: Timeless Lessons to Connect, Compete and Lead in Sports in Life. articles.description: The armpit is the part of the body that is dark, stinky, and unattractive. Everyone has one, but no one wants to see it or acknowledge it, and would rather cover it up and move on. The armpit of American youth sports is the culture of win-at-all-costs, uneducated, over the top coaches and parents who…

articles.description: This past week on our Facebook page, we had a great discussion on how to praise your athletes, so that the praise would be helpful, and not harmful (yes, it can be harmful) to their development. It was really a great discussion, so I wanted to sum it all up for everyone so you had…

articles.title: Why Volunteer Coaches Need to be More Like Volunteer Firefighters - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Volunteer firefighters sped hours preparing for the moment they are called upon to save a life. Why dont we ask the same of our volunteer coaches?

I speak to tens of thousands of people about youth sports, coaching, and athlete development. Every time I do a live event, I get asked the following question: “If you are presenting all this…

articles.title: Don't Neglect the Small Things in 2013 - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: It’s getting close to that time of year when we resolve to make positive changes in the upcoming year: New Year’s Resolution time! Here is a resolution to add to your list. It is not one that will help you lose weight or get fit physically, but it can make all the difference in building…

articles.title: My Kid Stopped Trying! What Now? - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: One of the questions I am often asked is “How do I change my kid’s attitude toward sports? He just does not seem into it anymore, and he is not putting any effort in.” This is a situation that can be very confusing and discouraging for both children and parents, as well as cause stress…

articles.title: The Difference Between Winning and Losing - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: By James Leath (this first appeared on his blog at www.JamesLeath.com) A former student athlete of mine was awarded a full ride to play NCAA D1 football and he called me recently, just to talk. Calls from former athletes are a huge highlight in any coach’s day. “Coach, what is the difference between winning and…

articles.title: The Ride Home - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: One of the saddest things I had to do as a Director of Coaching for numerous soccer clubs was conduct exit interviews, meetings with players whom had decided to leave the club. Children quit sports for a litany of reasons, and my job was always to see what we could learn, so we could improve…

articles.title: The Tipping Point in Youth Sports - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: “What happens to us parents and coaches,” I often get asked, “that turns us from sensible, relaxed people to stressed out adults roaming up and down sports sidelines and screaming at every play?” It is a question I ponder a lot, especially as I often get to watch my young children’s games played side by…

articles.title: Two Magic Words - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Far too many coaches and parents on our sidelines are micromanaging performance and stealing our players reps, yet EVERY ONE OF THEM SAW THE PLAY FROM A COMPLETELY DIFFERENT PERSPECTIVE AS THE ATHLETE! Try these two words instead.

articles.title: 3 Questions That Turn Losing into Learning - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: When kids lose in youth sports, bad things can happen. Angry moms and dads berate their kids from the sidelines and on the ride home after games. Coaches yell and scream at athletes, or worse yet, don’t even let some kids play in matches because they fear losing. We teach kids to fear losing, instead of learn from it. Here are three questions to ask your kids after every loss.

articles.title: Should My Child Play Up? The Do's and Don'ts of Moving Kids to Older Age Groups - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Should a young athlete play up in age against more advanced players, or play against chronological peers? We discuss the dos and don'ts of playing up.

articles.title: Faster, Higher, Stronger: An Interview with Mark McClusky - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Every day the limits of the human body and being pushed. articles.description: With college coaches lining the sidelines of middle school age games, and ninth graders making verbal commitments, it's time the NCAA stopped the insanity!

articles.title: Why I Love High School Sports - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Two weeks ago, my son TJ’s (#9 above with his senior teammates) high school soccer career came to an end in a state playoff game, culminating a week of highs and lows for me, of beginnings and endings, of joy and pain. All of those things come together in high school sports and that’s why…

articles.title: 2023 Most Important Books of the Year - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: It’s that time of year for us to share our favorite, most important books of 2023. We were able to interview some amazing authors this year, and read so, so many good books, but a few books really stood out to us as exceptional. Many of these books have been impactful on both a personal…

articles.title: An Open Letter from the Back Seat - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Many children say the ride home after the game is their worst memory of youth sports. Here is what the mindset of a child might be like in your back seat.

articles.title: Jordan Spieth's Multi-Sport Path to Golf Stardom - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: On June 21, at the ripe old age of 21, Jordan Spieth become just the sixth golfer in history to win both The Masters and The US Open in the same year, joining some pretty illustrious company such as Woods, Nicklaus, Palmer and Hogan.  He must have played only golf growing up to become this…

articles.title: Our 2019 Books of the Year - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: “The only thing that will prevent you from getting better is thinking that you know it all.” That was the advice Fergus Connelly gave us on our podcast this year, and it is a great concept to think about as 2019 draws to a close. So how do you get better? Well, reading books and…

articles.title: The Best Sports Books of 2018 - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Every year we scour the globe for the best books. Here is our list for 2018 of the top books for parents, coaches and athletes. Enjoy.

articles.title: The Problems We Face Part III: Values - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Today’s children need youth sports to teach them valuable life lessons and core values more than they have ever needed them in history.  As recently as 15 years ago, popular television and music still exemplified many family values that we would consider, positive, such as friendship, caring, and respect, but that has all changed.  The…

articles.title: Chapters: How it Feels to NOT Make the Olympic Team - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Cassidy Lichtman is a member of the USA Womens Volleyball team, and recently learned that she would not be going to the 2016 Rio Olympics. Here are her inspirational thoughts on the pain, the disappointment, and the future.

articles.title: Don't Turn Your Young Athlete Into a Mini Me - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: “Dad, am I a disappointment to you?” My son asked, choking back tears. I stopped dead in my tracks. We were walking out of a local amusement park on our way to the car. We had spent the day at the park to bond. It seems the plan backfired. I gathered myself. Inside I was…

articles.title: Let's Stop the Early Sport Specialization Madness! - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: The reason kids are being asked to play a single sport has little to do with science and psychology, and more to do with the business of youth sports. It's time to end the madness.

articles.title: Saying Goodbye to My #1 Fan - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: On April 19, 2024, I said goodbye to my #1 fan. I said goodbye to the person who always told me "I love watching you play." To the person who believed in me no matter what, and always encouraged me to dream big, and to jump off the cliff and grow my wings on the way down. On April 19, my beautiful mom Kathleen O'Sullivan, known by all of us as Granny O, passed away. I am heartbroken.

articles.title: The Storm Has Its Purpose - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: The other day I was on a live webinar with a couple of dozen parents of young athletes, almost all moms. They were there to gain some insight into how they could best support their children on the sporting journey across a variety of sports. I was asked a question that is a very common…

articles.title: How Would You Spend $100 Million to Improve Your Favorite Sport? My Advice for US Soccer - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: US soccer supposedly has $100 million in the bank and is considering building a national training center. Here are 4 ways that money could be better spent.

articles.title: Choosing the Hard Right in Youth Sports - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: In 2021 we will need courage to choose the hard right in youth sports and work together to give sports back to our kids.

articles.title: Helping our Kids Deal with Injuries - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Throughout my coaching career, I got many emails and letters from parents, but one question I was asked often was “How do I deal with my child’s recent injury? He/she is emotional, scared, and unsure about the long road back to recovery.” This is a difficult time for any parent – and athlete – and…

articles.title: A Case for Nurture Over Nature - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: “Your daughter is so beautiful.” The kind woman said, smiling at my 18-year-old daughter. “Thanks, she got her mother’s looks.” I said reflexively. Then I cringed. That’s my “go to response”. Anytime I receive a compliment on my daughter’s looks, I immediately reply about her genetic connection to her mother. She did get her mom’s…

articles.title: Timeout! The Importance of Downtime for Youth Sports Families - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: “But dad, we have so much going on!” “Do we have to go mom? I want to play in another tournament?” “Will we miss out if we don’t do the extra private training?” Have you heard these types of sentiments before from your spouse or your children? I know I have. The past few weeks…

articles.title: Utah Referee’s Death is One More Example of the Need to Change the Game! - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: The recent tragic death of Utah youth soccer referee Ricardo Portillo has once again highlighted the need to make drastic changes in the youth sports environment. Portillo was killed after being punched in the face by a 17 year old player that had been cautioned on the previous play. Once again, the ugly side of…

articles.title: "Make Failure Your Fuel": How to Help Your Athletes Learn from Adversity - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Abby Wambach delivered a great message to the graduates of Barnard College: 'Make Failure Your Fuel." Here are 5 ways you can help your athletes with adversity

articles.title: The Great Trophy Debate: Do We Need Participation Awards? - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: “Daddy, do we get trophies for playing lacrosse this spring?” my son asked me a few months back. “No, you get to go out and run around and have lots of fun while learning lacrosse,” I said. “OK!” Then he turned and ran out on the field for the start of his first practice. According…

articles.title: The Rocky Road of Excellence - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: “How many of you want to be a college athlete?” I asked this question to 3,000 middle school students in Southern California recently. In all, about 1,000 kids raised their hands. “What about an artist? A singer? What about a musician?” Maybe 500 students raised their hands. “What about running a business? Raise your hand…

articles.title: Why are so Many Girls Tearing Their A.C.L.s? - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Today's girls have a one in six chance of tearing their ACL and a one in three chance of re-tearing it if they are injured. Can we prevent this?

articles.title: Can You Over-Sample in Youth Sports? - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Every month we get interesting emails and messages from our Changing the Game family, and once in a while we get one that is worth throwing to the community. We got an email from a set of parents, both former NCAA Division I athletes, with three young children highly involved in sports. As avid readers…

articles.title: A Final Game of H-O-R-S-E - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: In our rush to get "10,000 hours" and do more at younger ages, are we taking the time to slow down, play catch, shoot hoops, and simply be present for our kids?

articles.title: Our Children Become the Messages They Hear the Most - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Under pressure, athletes self-talk can make or break their performance. What does that voice in their head say? According to expert Dr. Jim Taylor, kids become the messages they hear the most.

articles.title: Sports Skills do Not Always 'Just Happen!' - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Sports Parenting is an art, and for each and every child and family, there are a variety of parenting styles, methods and ideas that can all lead to the outcome of a positive sports experience, and the creation of a life long athlete.   Most of us rely upon our own athletic experiences to guide us with…

articles.title: It's Time to End the Sideline Sportsanity - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: As soon as I stepped out of my car in the parking lot, I could hear it. It was a beautiful Saturday afternoon of travel soccer, but there it was. You know what I’m talking about: that sometimes beautiful, often times excruciating cacophony of sounds that we have come to know as “cheering for our…

articles.title: “When I’m Running, it Feels like I am not Disabled:” Lessons on Being a Great Teammate from the Best Team You've Never Heard Of - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: Three great lessons in teamwork from the most unlikely team of all, Dick and Rick Hoyt

articles.title: Youth Sports is a Journey, Not a Destination - Changing the Game Project
articles.description: All too often youth sports becomes a destination, and we forget about the big picture. When we do that, we waste our precious moments with our children.
